coredata.dc:title: Strong coupling effect at the interface of cobalt phosphate-carbon dots boost photocatalytic water splitting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Mengmeng
Han, Mumei
Zhu, Cheng
Hu, Lulu
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen and oxygen produced by water splitting under solar energy are ideal future energy sources. At present, obtaining the efficient, stable and inexpensive photocatalyst for photocatalytic overall water splitting is still a huge challenge. Cobalt phosphate (Co3PO4, CoPi) possesses proper band positions for water splitting. However, the fast recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs for CoPi restricts its application. Herein, strongly coupling Co3PO4-carbon dots (CoPi-CDs) composite was constructed as an effective strategy to depress the fast recombination behavior of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. CoPi-CDs show superior photocatalytic water splitting activity than that of single CoPi. When the concentration of CDs in the composite is 0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst, the hydrogen production rate was obtained for approximately 0.592 μmol h−1, as well as the oxygen evolution rate about 0.258 μmol h−1 (with 2:1 stoichiometry), which are both nearly 33 times than that of pristine CoPi. This enhanced photocatalytic activity of CoPi-CDs should ascribe to the efficient coupling effect between CoPi and CDs, which allows fast electron transfer at the interface of CoPi and CDs and thus effectively boosts the photocatalytic water splitting. The strongly coupling nanocomposites should be inspiring for further nanocomposite building for photocatalytic overall water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co3PO4-carbon dots composite
Strong coupling
Facilitated electron transfer
Interface
Photocatalytic overall water splitting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 51572179 , 21471106 , 21771132 , 21501126 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20161216 ) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) SEM images, (c and d) TEM and HRTEM images, (e) SAED pattern of CoPi-CDs composite. (f) XRD spectra of CoPi-CDs composite and standard CoPi.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Co 2p, (b) P 2p, (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s for CoPi-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoPi (black curve) and CoPi-CDs (red curve). (b) (αhʋ)1/2 versus hʋ curve of CoPi (black curve) and CoPi-CDs (red curve). (c) UPS spectrum of CoPi-CDs. (d) Band structure diagram of CoPi-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Dependence of evolution of H2 and O2 from water on time course under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm), catalysed by (a) CoPi and (b) CoPi-CDs (0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst). (c) Amount of gases evolution of CoPi-CDs with different quantity of CDs. (d) The cyclic stability of CoPi-CDs (0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst) for photocatalytic water splitting under visible light.

(a) Photoresponse curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs (0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst). (b) EIS curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs. (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of H2O2 calibration curve and tested curve for CoPi-CDs after 12 h light irradiation of λ > 420 nm. (d) The i–t curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs under illumination.
The photocatalytic mechanism for overall water splitting over the CoPi-CDs photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Strong coupling effect at the interface of cobalt phosphate-carbon dots boost photocatalytic water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: M. Z. and M. H. contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strongly coupling CoPi-CDs composite exhibits effective and stable photocatalytic activity towards overall water splitting.

Hydrogen and oxygen produced by water splitting under solar energy are ideal future energy sources. At present, obtaining the efficient, stable and inexpensive photocatalyst for photocatalytic overall water splitting is still a huge challenge. Cobalt phosphate (Co3PO4, CoPi) possesses proper band positions for water splitting. However, the fast recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs for CoPi restricts its application. Herein, strongly coupling Co3PO4-carbon dots (CoPi-CDs) composite was constructed as an effective strategy to depress the fast recombination behavior of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. CoPi-CDs show superior photocatalytic water splitting activity than that of single CoPi. When the concentration of CDs in the composite is 0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst, the hydrogen production rate was obtained for approximately 0.592 μmol h−1, as well as the oxygen evolution rate about 0.258 μmol h−1 (with 2:1 stoichiometry), which are both nearly 33 times than that of pristine CoPi. This enhanced photocatalytic activity of CoPi-CDs should ascribe to the efficient coupling effect between CoPi and CDs, which allows fast electron transfer at the interface of CoPi and CDs and thus effectively boosts the photocatalytic water splitting. The strongly coupling nanocomposites should be inspiring for further nanocomposite building for photocatalytic overall water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen and oxygen have become significant members of the promising clean and environmental-friendly energy sources. Photocatalytic water splitting is one of the valid methods to produce hydrogen and oxygen via sunlight and effective photocatalysts without pollutant by-products [1–7]. At present, obtaining the efficient, stable and inexpensive photocatalyst for photocatalytic over water splitting is still a huge challenge.

Recently, cobalt phosphate (Co3PO4, CoPi) has attracted much attention due to its cost-effective, high natural abundance, non-toxicity and environmental compatibility [8,9]. In the past decade, CoPi has been demonstrated to work effectively as oxygen-evolving electrocatalyst [9]. Besides, many semiconductors (such as α–Fe3O4, ZnO, WO3, Si, BiVO4, etc.) have integrated with CoPi to improve their performance [10–14]. For example, Wang etc. designed a CoPi-ZnO composite using as photocatalyst for water oxidation, where CoPi acted as cocatalyst [11]. Apart from acting as oxygen evolution electrocatalyst and cocatalyst, CoPi possesses proper band positions for water splitting [15–18]. However, the fast recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs of CoPi greatly hinders its application.

As a rising star in photocatalysis, carbon dots (CDs) with their unique optical and electrical properties have caused the extensive research [19,20]. For the past few years, several articles have been demonstrated that CDs could exactly improve the photocatalytic activity of pristine materials [20,21]. For example, Kang’s group constructed the CoO-CDs composite showing enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability for tetracycline degradation under visible light irradiation. And the improvement could be ascribed to the strengthened interaction between CDs and CoO and the enhanced charge separation efficiency caused by CDs [21]. Besides, they showed the CdS-CDs composite is used as photocatalyst for overall water splitting and exhibits significant photocatalytic water splitting and stability compared with that of CdS [22]. The above reveals that the incorporation of CDs in photocatalysts can enhance the absorbance of visible light, accelerate the electron transfer and separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, hence improve the photocatalytic properties of catalysts. Therefore, in combination with CDs and CoPi to construct the CoPi-CDs composite may efficiently reduce the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and enhance the photocatalytic activity of CoPi for overall water splitting.

Herein, strongly coupling CoPi-carbon dots (CoPi-CDs) composite was constructed as an effective strategy to depress the fast recombination behavior of photogenerated electron and hole pairs. The designed CoPi-CDs composites show preferable photocatalytic activity with impressive stability for overall water splitting without requirement for any sacrificial agents or others cocatalysts. When the concentration of CDs in the composite is 0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst, the hydrogen production rate was obtained for approximately 0.592 μmol h−1, as well as the oxygen evolution rate about 0.258 μmol h−1 (with 2:1 stoichiometry), which are both nearly 33 times than that of pristine CoPi. This enhanced photocatalytic activity of CoPi-CDs should ascribe to the efficient coupling effect between CoPi and CDs, which allows fast electron transfer at the interface of CoPi and CDs. As a result, photogenerated electron in the CoPi was transferred and redistributed at the surface of CDs, which favorably hampered the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs and effectively boosted the photocatalytic water splitting. The strongly coupling nanocomposites should be inspiring for further nanocomposite building for photocatalytic water splitting.

CDs were synthesized according to our previous report [19]. Briefly, two graphic rods (after treatment) were inserted into the ultrapure water as the electrodes. A bias of 30 V was applied on the electrodes. The anode graphic rod was corroded gradually, and the solution got darker over time. The final solution was filtrated with double-layered filter paper and then treated with a freeze-drying way to get the CDs power.

0.873 g (∼3 mmol) Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved into 100 mL ultrapure water under constant stirring. Then, the quantitative CDs were added into the solution. After that, 10 mL diammonium phosphate solution (containing 2 mmol diammonium phosphate) was injected to the mentioned above mixture solution dropwise through a microsyringe at room temperature. After the injection finished, the resultant solution was kept stirring for 2 h and finally obtaining pink precipitate. The precipitate was then centrifuged, washed and dried to collect the CoPi-CDs composite. The CoPi was synthesized through the similar procedure in absence of CDs.

The photocatalytic performance of CoPi-CDs was evaluated through a multi-channel photocatalytic reaction system (White light 5 W, PCX50A Discover, Beijing PerfectLight technology Co. Ltd, China). In a typical experiment, 25 mg sample (CoPi-CDs) was dissolved into 25 mL ultrapure water. The mixture was then transferred to a photoreactor vial (50 mL) with a magneton on the bottom of the vial. Under constant stirring, a white light-emitting diode (420 nm ≤ λ < 700 nm) was used as the visible light source. A gas chromatograph (GC-7900 T, 5 Å molecular sieve column, TCD detector) was used to detect the gas production.

The UV–vis spectroscopy was used to detect whether H2O2 can form during the photocatalytic process with o-tolidine as the peroxide indicator. After the same operation in the photocatalytic water splitting, the reaction suspension was centrifuged to collect 3 mL liquid supernatant. Then 1 mL 1% o-tolidine in 0.1 M HCl was added to the supernatant. Finally, the UV–vis spectroscopy was used to measure the absorbance of the supernatant and the concentration of H2O2 was estimated through the characteristic peak at 436 nm, which is the typical peak generated by H2O2.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a FEI quanta 200 microscope with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and High-angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) were measured by using a FEI-Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out to characterize the samples using a PIXcel3D X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). XPS measurement was carried out with a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were performed with He I (21.22 eV) as the monochromatic light source. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 760E workstation with a standard three-electrode system, of which a platinum wire is used as the reference electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the counter electrode and a glass carbon (GC)electrode as working electrode. The photoresponse–time curves were obtained at open circuit potential (OCP) in 0.1 M sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) with a 300 W Xe-lamp as the light source. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were carried out with a frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz in ultrapure water.

The electron transfer number (n) was evaluated according to the following equation [22]:
n =
4
I d
I d
+
I r
/ N
where Id and Ir represent the disk and ring current, respectively, while N is the RRDE collection efficiency which equals to 0.24.

The SEM, TEM and HRTEM images were employed to investigate the morphology and structure of CoPi-CDs composites. Fig. 1 a and b give the SEM images of CoPi-CDs composite, which present hierarchical CoPi-CDs microflowers with average diameter of 5–7 µm assembly by several nanoflakes. The TEM and HRTEM images of CoPi-CDs composites under different scales are provided in Fig. 1c and d, which indicate that CDs are distributed on the surface of the porous nanoflakes. The illustration in Fig. 1d displays the HRTEM image of a single CD particle with the crystal lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, assigning to (1 0 0) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. [22] Besides, Fig. S1 exhibits the SEM image of pure CoPi, which presents similar morphology with CoPi-CDs composite and indicates that CDs do not influence the morphology of CoPi. The CoPi-CDs composite was further characterized with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 1e. As shown, the measured interplanar spacings from the SAED pattern are 4.88, 3.86, and 2.98 Å corresponding to the (2 0 0), (20-1), and (2 0 1) planes of monoclinic Co3(PO4)2·8H2O. [16,23] In addition, HAADF-STEM along with the elemental mapping was employed to characterize the CoPi-CDs composite (Fig. S2), which shows that C is distributed on the surface of the composite. XRD pattern for CoPi-CDs composite is provided in Fig. 1d, where the peaks of CoPi-CDs composite are consistent with the JCPDS NO. 41-0375, revealing that the CoPi was synthesized and the addition of CDs didn’t affect the phase structure of CoPi. It's worth noting that the XRD peak of CDs is not observed in the CoPi-CDs, possibly due to the trace amount of CDs in the composite or the overlap with the CoPi peaks. The corresponding Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectra are given in Fig. S3. The above characterization results provide evidence that CoPi-CDs composites were synthesized.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.06.078.

The SEM, TEM and HRTEM images were employed to investigate the morphology and structure of CoPi-CDs composites. Fig. 1 a and b give the SEM images of CoPi-CDs composite, which present hierarchical CoPi-CDs microflowers with average diameter of 5–7 µm assembly by several nanoflakes. The TEM and HRTEM images of CoPi-CDs composites under different scales are provided in Fig. 1c and d, which indicate that CDs are distributed on the surface of the porous nanoflakes. The illustration in Fig. 1d displays the HRTEM image of a single CD particle with the crystal lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, assigning to (1 0 0) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. [22] Besides, Fig. S1 exhibits the SEM image of pure CoPi, which presents similar morphology with CoPi-CDs composite and indicates that CDs do not influence the morphology of CoPi. The CoPi-CDs composite was further characterized with the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 1e. As shown, the measured interplanar spacings from the SAED pattern are 4.88, 3.86, and 2.98 Å corresponding to the (2 0 0), (20-1), and (2 0 1) planes of monoclinic Co3(PO4)2·8H2O. [16,23] In addition, HAADF-STEM along with the elemental mapping was employed to characterize the CoPi-CDs composite (Fig. S2), which shows that C is distributed on the surface of the composite. XRD pattern for CoPi-CDs composite is provided in Fig. 1d, where the peaks of CoPi-CDs composite are consistent with the JCPDS NO. 41-0375, revealing that the CoPi was synthesized and the addition of CDs didn’t affect the phase structure of CoPi. It's worth noting that the XRD peak of CDs is not observed in the CoPi-CDs, possibly due to the trace amount of CDs in the composite or the overlap with the CoPi peaks. The corresponding Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectra are given in Fig. S3. The above characterization results provide evidence that CoPi-CDs composites were synthesized.

The XPS spectra were measured to analyse the element composition of CoPi-CDs composite. Fig. S4 reveals the full XPS survey spectrum of the CoPi-CDs composite. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p, P 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are shown in Fig. 2 . The Co 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 2a, which can be fitted into two individual peaks Co 2p3/2, Co 2p1/2 at 781.3 eV and 797.2 eV with two satellites. [16] The peaks at 133.0 eV and 134.1 eV in Fig. 2b correspond to the P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the phosphorus atoms with phosphate groups in CoPi-CDs composite. [16] Fig. 3 c displays the O 1s spectrum of CoPi-CDs composite, which can be fitted into three peaks. These peaks located at 530.7 eV, 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV can be assigned to the CoOC, CO and CO bonds in CoPi-CDs composite, indicating the strong coupling effect between CoPi and CDs. [24,25] The C 1s spectrum of CoPi-CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. As shown, the C 1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 284.6, 286.3, and 288.6 eV, which can be attributed to the CC, CO and CO bonds from CDs. [21,22] The XPS results indicate that the strongly coupling CoPi-CDs composite was synthesized successfully.

The XPS spectra were measured to analyse the element composition of CoPi-CDs composite. Fig. S4 reveals the full XPS survey spectrum of the CoPi-CDs composite. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p, P 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are shown in Fig. 2 . The Co 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 2a, which can be fitted into two individual peaks Co 2p3/2, Co 2p1/2 at 781.3 eV and 797.2 eV with two satellites. [16] The peaks at 133.0 eV and 134.1 eV in Fig. 2b correspond to the P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the phosphorus atoms with phosphate groups in CoPi-CDs composite. [16] Fig. 3 c displays the O 1s spectrum of CoPi-CDs composite, which can be fitted into three peaks. These peaks located at 530.7 eV, 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV can be assigned to the CoOC, CO and CO bonds in CoPi-CDs composite, indicating the strong coupling effect between CoPi and CDs. [24,25] The C 1s spectrum of CoPi-CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. As shown, the C 1s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 284.6, 286.3, and 288.6 eV, which can be attributed to the CC, CO and CO bonds from CDs. [21,22] The XPS results indicate that the strongly coupling CoPi-CDs composite was synthesized successfully.

The optical properties of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite were examined through UV–vis adsorption spectra exhibited in Fig. 3a. The UV–vis absorption spectra of pristine CoPi (black curve) and CoPi-CDs composite (red curve) both show an absorption peak at about 520 nm, which is beneficial to absorb visible light. The optical band gaps of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite can be estimated from the Tauc plot, which are derived from the UV–vis absorption spectra. The corresponding calculation is based on the following equation [26]:
α = A
h υ -
E g
n / 2
/ h υ
where α, A, h, ʋ and Eg correspond to the absorption coefficient, proportionality constant, Planck’s constant, frequency of the incident light and band energy, respectively (n in the index is equal to 1 for direct band gap, 4 for indirect band gap (here)). [16] The switched Tauc plots of the (αhʋ)1/2 versus photon energy (hʋ) are displayed in Fig. 3b. Thus, the band gap energy (Eg) of CoPi-CDs was determined to be 1.88 eV, which is slightly smaller than that of the pristine CoPi (1.93 eV, Fig. 3b, black curve), indicating that the strongly coupling CoPi-CDs composite is easier to harvest more solar energy and generate more electron-hole pairs.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was performed to determine the ionization potential (equal to the valence band energy (EVB)) and explore the band positions of CoPi-CDs. The valance band energy (EV) of CoPi-CDs was calculated to be 6.26 eV vs vacuum, by subtracting the width of the He I UPS spectrum from the excitation energy (21.22 eV). And thus, the conduction band (EC) was determined to be 4.38 eV (Ec = Ev − Eg). Consequently, Fig. 3d displays the energy level diagram of CoPi-CDs, which clearly shows that the CB level of CoPi-CDs is positioned above the reduction level for H+/H2 (0 V vs. RHE), and the VB level is located below the oxidation level for O2/H2O (1.23 eV vs. RHE). Therefore, the CoPi-CDs composite has an appropriate band position to realize the overall water splitting.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs are given in Fig. S5, which can be used to investigate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CoPi and CoPi-CDs. The HOMO and LUMO levels are calculated according to the following equations [27]:

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs are given in Fig. S5, which can be used to investigate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CoPi and CoPi-CDs. The HOMO and LUMO levels are calculated according to the following equations [27]:

E
LUMO
= -
(
E
onset
red
-
E
ferrocene
+
4.8
)
eV
where
E
onset
ox
,
E
onset
red
and
E
ferrocene
are the onset of oxidation, reduction potential of the catalyst, and the oxidation potential of ferrocene, respectively. Therefore, the calculated HOMO/LUMO energy levels for CoPi are −6.15 and −4.26 eV. And that for CoPi-CDs are −6.04 and −4.17 eV. Obviously, both CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite have proper band positions for water splitting to generate O2 and H2. Besides, the addition of CDs doesn't make a significant change to the band position of CoPi. The results are consistent with the optical band gap above.

The photocatalytic overall water splitting reactions over the as-prepared samples were evaluated under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) without the aid of applied bias potential or any sacrificial agents or others cocatalysts. Fig. 4 a and b exhibit the gases evolution for CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite, respectively. As shown, both the CoPi and optimized CoPi-CDs composite (0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst) display the evolutions of H2 and O2. H2 and O2 were both quantified by gas chromatography (GC). The maximum H2 production rate for the optimized CoPi-CDs composite was calculated to be 0.592 µmol h−1, while the O2 production rate reaches 0.258 µmol h−1 (Fig. 4b), which are both almost 33-fold larger than that of the pure CoPi (0.018 and 0.008 µmol h−1, respectively, Fig. 4a). In addition, the photocatalytic activity of CDs was performed and the no hydrogen signal could be observed for single CDs component after photocatalytic reaction, which indicates the CDs can use as cocatalyst in the photocatalytic system. The concentration of CDs in the composite was further adjusted to explore the influence of the amount of CDs (Fig. 4c). For pure CoPi, low amount of H2 (0.018 µmol h−1) was detected. However, for CoPi-CDs composite, the H2 production markedly enhanced and reached the highest value when CDs concentration increased from 0 to 0.002 (gCDs/gcatalyst). The improved photocatalytic property reveals that the strong coupling effect between CDs and CoPi plays an important role in the composite, which allows charge transfer at the interface between CoPi and CDs and thus promotes the separation and diffusion of charge carriers. However, excess CDs led to a low H2 evolution and O2 evolution, which may due to over-occupied of CDs on the CoPi. The stability of CoPi-CDs towards water splitting was further investigated through cyclic stability experiments, which is displayed in Fig. 4d. The CoPi-CDs composite exhibits a good stability with reused times reaching 7 times. For each time, the radiation time is 24 h, and the catalyst exhibits a slight loss after 7th reuse. The XRD pattern and high resolution XPS spectra of CoPi-CDs composite after long-term photocatalytic reaction were supplied in Figs. S6 and S7. As shown, after photocatalytic reaction, the XRD pattern of CoPi-CDs composite remains almost the same, which indicates that the crystalline structure of CoPi-CDs composite is unchanged. The XPS spectra shown in Fig. S7 further confirm the good stability of CoPi-CDs composite since the high-resolution spectra of CoPi-CDs composite after photocatalytic reaction are almost unchanged compared with that of pristine CoPi-CDs composite.

The photocatalytic overall water splitting reactions over the as-prepared samples were evaluated under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) without the aid of applied bias potential or any sacrificial agents or others cocatalysts. Fig. 4 a and b exhibit the gases evolution for CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite, respectively. As shown, both the CoPi and optimized CoPi-CDs composite (0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst) display the evolutions of H2 and O2. H2 and O2 were both quantified by gas chromatography (GC). The maximum H2 production rate for the optimized CoPi-CDs composite was calculated to be 0.592 µmol h−1, while the O2 production rate reaches 0.258 µmol h−1 (Fig. 4b), which are both almost 33-fold larger than that of the pure CoPi (0.018 and 0.008 µmol h−1, respectively, Fig. 4a). In addition, the photocatalytic activity of CDs was performed and the no hydrogen signal could be observed for single CDs component after photocatalytic reaction, which indicates the CDs can use as cocatalyst in the photocatalytic system. The concentration of CDs in the composite was further adjusted to explore the influence of the amount of CDs (Fig. 4c). For pure CoPi, low amount of H2 (0.018 µmol h−1) was detected. However, for CoPi-CDs composite, the H2 production markedly enhanced and reached the highest value when CDs concentration increased from 0 to 0.002 (gCDs/gcatalyst). The improved photocatalytic property reveals that the strong coupling effect between CDs and CoPi plays an important role in the composite, which allows charge transfer at the interface between CoPi and CDs and thus promotes the separation and diffusion of charge carriers. However, excess CDs led to a low H2 evolution and O2 evolution, which may due to over-occupied of CDs on the CoPi. The stability of CoPi-CDs towards water splitting was further investigated through cyclic stability experiments, which is displayed in Fig. 4d. The CoPi-CDs composite exhibits a good stability with reused times reaching 7 times. For each time, the radiation time is 24 h, and the catalyst exhibits a slight loss after 7th reuse. The XRD pattern and high resolution XPS spectra of CoPi-CDs composite after long-term photocatalytic reaction were supplied in Figs. S6 and S7. As shown, after photocatalytic reaction, the XRD pattern of CoPi-CDs composite remains almost the same, which indicates that the crystalline structure of CoPi-CDs composite is unchanged. The XPS spectra shown in Fig. S7 further confirm the good stability of CoPi-CDs composite since the high-resolution spectra of CoPi-CDs composite after photocatalytic reaction are almost unchanged compared with that of pristine CoPi-CDs composite.

The time-current (i-t) curves for CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite were also recorded to estimate the photoresponse properties with the period of light on/off every 50 s. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CoPi-CDs composite exhibits enhanced photocurrent intensity compared with that of pure CoPi, which indicates an increased charge separation rate of CoPi-CDs composite. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was used to investigate the charge transfer ability of the photocatalysts. Fig. 5b describes the EIS Nyquist plots of CoPi and CoPi-CDs obtained in pure water. Inserted graph in Fig. 5b is the electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) comprising a series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and constant phase element. [28] The charge transfer resistance value for CoPi is 13 kΩ and for CoPi-CDs is 8802 Ω. Therefore, CoPi-CDs composite displays a smaller charge transfer resistance than that of the CoPi. What’s more, UV–vis absorbance spectrum was used to detect whether H2O2 was produced or not during the water splitting process (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. 5c, no obvious signal of H2O2 was detected for CoPi-CDs composite, which indicates that no H2O2 was generated during water splitting. Fig. 5d displays the i-t curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite under illumination, where no obvious photocurrent oscillations (which may be caused by the cycles of H2O2 generation, adsorption, decomposition by catalysts and diffusion) was observed for the photocatalytic activity of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite. [26] The RRDE curves displayed in Fig. S8 were used to determine the electron transfer number (n) of CoPi-CDs, where n was calculated to be 4. Therefore, the photocatalytic process of CoPi-CDs composite is a four-electron pathway.

The time-current (i-t) curves for CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite were also recorded to estimate the photoresponse properties with the period of light on/off every 50 s. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the CoPi-CDs composite exhibits enhanced photocurrent intensity compared with that of pure CoPi, which indicates an increased charge separation rate of CoPi-CDs composite. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was used to investigate the charge transfer ability of the photocatalysts. Fig. 5b describes the EIS Nyquist plots of CoPi and CoPi-CDs obtained in pure water. Inserted graph in Fig. 5b is the electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) comprising a series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and constant phase element. [28] The charge transfer resistance value for CoPi is 13 kΩ and for CoPi-CDs is 8802 Ω. Therefore, CoPi-CDs composite displays a smaller charge transfer resistance than that of the CoPi. What’s more, UV–vis absorbance spectrum was used to detect whether H2O2 was produced or not during the water splitting process (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. 5c, no obvious signal of H2O2 was detected for CoPi-CDs composite, which indicates that no H2O2 was generated during water splitting. Fig. 5d displays the i-t curves of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite under illumination, where no obvious photocurrent oscillations (which may be caused by the cycles of H2O2 generation, adsorption, decomposition by catalysts and diffusion) was observed for the photocatalytic activity of CoPi and CoPi-CDs composite. [26] The RRDE curves displayed in Fig. S8 were used to determine the electron transfer number (n) of CoPi-CDs, where n was calculated to be 4. Therefore, the photocatalytic process of CoPi-CDs composite is a four-electron pathway.

Based on all the results above, a possible mechanism for the CoPi-CDs composites in photocatalytic overall water splitting under visible light was proposed as shown in Scheme 1 . Under light radiation, the CoPi could absorb visible light and generate photo-induced electron-hole pairs. However, the generated electrons and holes will recombine with each other quickly. For the strongly coupling CoPi-CDs composite, due to the electron transfer behavior at the interface between CDs and CoPi, the photoinduced electrons in the conduction band of CoPi were redistributed on the surfaces of CDs, which inhibit the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. Thus, the H2 evolution at the interface of CoPi-CDs composite was greatly enhanced than that of CoPi. While the photoinduced holes in the valence band of CoPi would produce the O2 to realize the overall water splitting.

We have synthesized the Co3PO4-carbon dots composite through a facile precipitation method for photocatalytic overall water splitting without the requirement for any sacrificial agents or others cocatalysts. The resulting composite exhibits a great enhancement in photocatalytic activity and stability. When the concentration of CDs in the composite is 0.002 gCDs/gcatalyst, the hydrogen production rate was obtained for about 0.592 μmol h−1, with the oxygen evolution for about 0.258 μmol h−1, which are almost 33 times than that of pristine Co3PO4 and comparable with the reported Co3PO4-α-Fe2O3 Z-scheme photocatalyst. [16] This enhanced photocatalytic activity of Co3PO4-CDs should ascribe to the efficient coupling effect between Co3PO4 and CDs, which allows fast electron transfer at the interface of Co3PO4 and CDs. As a consequence, photogenerated electron in the Co3PO4 was transferred and redistributed at the surface of CDs, which favorably hampered the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs and effectively boosted the photocatalytic water splitting. The strongly coupling nanocomposites should be inspiring for further nanocomposite building for photocatalytic water splitting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 51572179, 21471106, 21771132, 21501126), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161216) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
